title: Advanced Customization via the NIMBLE MCMC Engine
text: Now, execute NIMBLE engine manually. explicitly, freedom intercept configuration object apply custom block samplers change hyperparameters calling nimbleMCMC(). Note sake testing/tutorial, code defaults fast compilation format. rigorous real-world analysis, might run 10,000 iterations across 2-3 chains! enabled summary WAIC flags function call , nimbleMCMC() conclude sampling process return composite list mcmc_raw object containing three primary elements: samples: mcmc.list containing raw, point--point posterior draws every tracked parameter across chains. summary: pre-calculated matrix providing mean, median, standard deviation, key credible intervals every estimated parameter. WAIC (Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion): estimator --sample predictive accuracy. Lower WAIC values indicate better-fitting model, highly useful comparing competing Q-Matrices network architectures. pWAIC: estimated “effective number parameters.” complex hierarchical Bayesian networks like cognitive modeling, penalty term describes structural complexity shrinkage model, penalizing overly parameterized networks risk overfitting. nimbleMCMC() convenience wrapper used handles model building compilation internally. even finer control-swapping individual parameter samplers-can use NIMBLE’s step--step workflow: approach lets inspect modify exact sampler assigned parameter node compilation.
code: mcmc_raw <- nimbleMCMC(     code = model_code, # The NIMBLE code we extracted earlier     constants = config$constants, # Fixed constants mapped from the Q-Matrix     data = config$data, # The isolated observational vectors     inits = config$inits, # Starter values for the Markov chain     monitors = config$monitors, # Variables we want NIMBLE to track     nchains = 2,     niter = 2000,     nburnin = 500,     summary = TRUE,     samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE, # Force output to a list format compatible with CODA     WAIC = TRUE # Calculate the Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion ) mcmc_raw$WAIC WAIC: 29483.86 pWAIC: 1828.96 # 1. Build the model object model <- nimbleModel(     code = model_code,     constants = config$constants,     data = config$data,     inits = config$inits )  # 2. Create a default MCMC configuration mcmc_conf <- configureMCMC(model, monitors = config$monitors)  # 3. Inspect the current sampler assignments print(mcmc_conf$getSamplers())  # 4. Customize: e.g., replace a specific sampler with a slice sampler # mcmc_conf$removeSamplers("beta_root[1]") # mcmc_conf$addSampler(target = "beta_root[1]", type = "slice")  # 5. Build, compile, and run mcmc_built <- buildMCMC(mcmc_conf) cmodel <- compileNimble(model) cmcmc <- compileNimble(mcmc_built, project = model) samples <- runMCMC(cmcmc, niter = 2000, nburnin = 500, nchains = 2,                    samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE)

Extract original participant IDs for correct row mapping student_ids <- rownames(config$data$X)  # 3. Map Nimble parameters to human-readable names mapped_results <- map_pgdcm_parameters(     summary_mx = mcmc_summ,     config_obj = config,     student_names = student_ids )  # 4. Generate the final clean tables (Skill Profiles, Item Parameters) summary_tables <- generate_summary_tables(     mapped_results = mapped_results,     config_obj = config,     student_names = student_ids,     return_groups = TRUE # Enable this to explicitly extract skill profile clusters )  # Extract the skill profiles specifically skill_profiles <- summary_tables$skill_profiles group_patterns <- summary_tables$group_patterns # Assess classification accuracy against known true profiles accuracy_results <- assess_classification_accuracy(     skill_profiles = skill_profiles,     true_data = dtmr_true_profiles,     mapping_list = list(         "referent_units" = "referent_units",         "partitioning_iterating" = "partitioning_iterating",         "appropriateness" = "appropriateness",         "multiplicative_comparison" = "multiplicative_comparison"     ) )  # View the individual skill accuracies, Cohen's Kappa, and overall profile match rate print(accuracy_results$metrics) print(paste("Profile Correct Classification Rate:", accuracy_results$profile_accuracy))

what: 2. Preparing the Data and the Evidence Model
title: Beginner Tutorial: Introduction to Bayesian Modeling with PGDCM
text: Diagnostic Classification Models (DCMs) evaluate whether participants mastered specific skills assessment designed measure. estimate , model requires two key components: Observational Data (X): actual test responses participants, typically consisting 1s (correct) 0s (incorrect). forms N×JN \times J matrix, NN number participants JJ number questions (items). Q-Matrix (Q): evidence model specifying exact skills required answer question correctly. forms J×KJ \times K matrix, JJ number questions KK number skills (attributes). Specifically, entry 1 jj-th row kk-th column indicates kk-th skill required answer jj-th question, 0 indicates . Let’s load sample dataset Q-Matrix: always good practice verify dimensions inspect rows make sure everything loaded correctly: pgdcm package mathematically treats modern psychometric models probabilistic graphical models (specifically, Directed Acyclic Graphs Bayesian Networks). , must first convert standard Q-Matrix Directed Acyclic Graph (g) represents underlying node dependencies-.e., dependencies skills tasks/test items. QMatrix2iGraph() function handles graphical conversion automatically us. constructed core network graph, several model parameters must defined establish active modeling environment NIMBLE. includes validating topological constraints, isolating constants required nimble inference calculations, setting initial values model parameters. build_model_config() function handles heavy lifting. important note default, QMatrix2iGraph() assigns DINA (Deterministic Input, Noisy “” gate) compute type nodes. implies strict, non-compensatory cognitive framework-meaning student must possess required skills dictated Q-Matrix likely answer item correctly; mastering just one required skills provides additional benefit. assessment follows different theoretical framework, can specify graph construction time supplying compute argument QMatrix2iGraph(): compute = "dino" (Deterministic Input, Noisy “” gate): Assumes fully compensatory framework. , possessing least one required skills sufficient likely answer item correctly. compute = "dinm" (Deterministic Input, Noisy “Mixed”): Assumes proportional additive framework. model, additional required skill student masters incrementally increases probability answering correctly. downstream build_model_config() function reads compute type directly graph’s node attributes, need specify configuration stage.
code: X <- dtmr_data Q <- dtmr_qmatrix # X should be N (students) x J (items) dim(X) head(X[, 1:6]) # first 6 columns  # Q should be J (items) x K (skills) dim(Q) head(Q) # Restructure Matrix to an iGraph object g <- QMatrix2iGraph(Q)  # Link student responses to the Graph config <- build_model_config(g, X) # Example: use a compensatory framework instead g <- QMatrix2iGraph(Q, compute = "dino")

Therefore, standard workflow practice execute save chain results disk via saveRDS() locally, load post-model fitting inference analysis. ’s happening hood run_pgdcm_auto? hood, run_pgdcm_auto() handles several complex configurations don’t manually code . function configures MCMC algorithmic engine following default parameters: niter = 1000: total number MCMC samples draw per chain. nburnin = 100: number initial samples discard. MCMC algorithms take time traverse towards high-probability region; discarding early “burn-” samples ensures analyze parameters reached stable state. chains = 2: algorithm runs two independent sampling processes simultaneously ensure converge onto distribution. Additionally, standard comprehensive workflows evaluate viability model using simulation. run_pgdcm_auto arguments (prior_sims = NULL, post_sims = NULL) bypass default speed, critical components full Bayesian workflow: Prior Predictive Checking: seeing actual data, kind data model think see based purely initial parameter bounds (priors)? ensures initial limits logical mathematically impossible. Posterior Predictive Checking: network learns distribution, simulate student response patterns utilizing trained distributions. compare simulated response patterns real observational data. model accurately captured underlying psychometric properties, simulated data closely resemble real data.

text: recipes run DTMR dataset (dcmdata::dtmr_data). tables summarize results can see different structural assumptions affect model fit, item parameter estimates, classification accuracy. Access results: results$WAIC Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC) measures --sample predictive accuracy. Lower better. effective number parameters (pWAIC) penalizes model complexity; LPPD log pointwise predictive density. dataset, HO-DCM achieves lowest WAIC, suggesting general-ability-driven skill structure provides best balance fit complexity assessment. Access results: results$item_parameters - difficulty_mean column contains values. second lambda index (lambda[j, 2]) captures item difficulty. Higher values mean item harder overall. Comparing across models reveals structural assumptions shift difficulty calibration. Access results: results$item_parameters - discrimination_mean column contains values. first lambda index (lambda[j, 1]) estimates main-effect discrimination (slope) - well item differentiates students possess relevant skills . multidimensional models (DCM, AH-DCM, HO-DCM), effect split across skills specified Q-matrix, cross-model comparisons interpreted cautiously. Access results: results$skill_profiles contains posterior mastery probabilities student. ground-truth mastery profiles available (simulated validated datasets), can evaluate well model recovers using assess_classification_accuracy(). table reports per-skill accuracy overall profile-match rate. code: # Read the X matrix X <- read_excel("testdatafile.xlsx", sheet = 1) message(sprintf("Loaded X matrix: %d students × %d items", nrow(X), ncol(X) - 1))  # Drop the Subject column; remaining columns are the task (item) names task_names <- colnames(X)[-1]  # Build nodes from task names and push to Cytoscape Tasks2CytoNodes(task_names) # Fetch the active network from Cytoscape myNetwork1 <- pull_from_cytoscape(base.url = cytoPort)  # Extract clean node and edge tables using our utilities nodes_table1 <- get_NodesTable(myNetwork1) edges_table1 <- get_EdgesTable(myNetwork1)  # Check out what we got! head(nodes_table1) head(edges_table1)  # Save the network for later write_graph(myNetwork1, "Scenario1_network.graphml", format = "graphml")

code: library(dcmdata) library(nimble) library(pgdcm)  set.seed(2026) N <- nrow(dtmr_data) shuffled_idx <- sample(1:N)  # Shuffle data X_shuffled <- dtmr_data[shuffled_idx, ]  # Match the true profiles to the shuffled IDs matched_idx <- match(     as.character(X_shuffled[[1]]),     as.character(dtmr_true_profiles[[1]]) ) true_profiles_shuffled <- dtmr_true_profiles[matched_idx, ]  # Use string IDs as row names so MCMC output is labeled correctly rownames(X_shuffled) <- as.character(X_shuffled[[1]]) rownames(true_profiles_shuffled) <- as.character(true_profiles_shuffled[[1]])  # Split into Calibration (Year 1) and Scoring (Year 2) samples half <- floor(N / 2) X_calib <- X_shuffled[1:half, ] X_score <- X_shuffled[(half + 1):N, ]  true_calib <- true_profiles_shuffled[1:half, ] true_score <- true_profiles_shuffled[(half + 1):N, ]

title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: heart pgdcm package lies single, unified Bayesian network model defined file DiBelloBN.R. one piece NIMBLE code highly flexible: depending configure graph (.e., node types compute rules assign), can express standard Diagnostic Classification Model (DCM), Item Response Theory (IRT) model, Multidimensional IRT (MIRT) model, Higher-Order DCM (HO-DCM). vignette written readers want understand exactly model mathematically. walk : general equation governs every node network. three condensation rules (DINA, DINO, DINM) determine parent information combined. model specializes IRT, MIRT, DCM, HO-DCM depending graph topology. semantic meaning every estimated parameter.

what: 1. The Big Picture: Everything is a Bayesian Network
title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: pgdcm model Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) - network nodes connected directional arrows, cycles. two kinds nodes: Attribute nodes (latent/unobserved): represent skills, abilities, competencies directly see. example, “Addition” “Reading Comprehension.” want estimate whether student mastered . Task nodes (observed): represent actual test items (questions). can see whether student answered correctly (1) incorrectly (0). arrows (edges) graph encode dependency: arrow Attribute AA Task TT, means mastery AA influences probability answering TT correctly. arrow Attribute A1A_1 Attribute A2A_2, means A1A_1 prerequisite A2A_2. question every model must answer : Given pattern correct/incorrect responses observe, likely configuration latent skills student? graph acyclic, can always arrange nodes topological order - ordering every parent appears children. pgdcm package enforces automatically via enforce_topo_sort(). ordering critical means can process nodes left right: reach node, parents already defined. nodes ordered : α1,α2,…,αK⏟Attribute nodes,X1,X2,…,XJ⏟Task nodes \underbrace{\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_K}_{\text{Attribute nodes}} \;,\; \underbrace{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_J}_{\text{Task nodes}} KK number attributes JJ number items.

what: 2. The Universal Equation: Logistic Regression at Every Node
title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: Every node network (whether attribute task) follows fundamental equation. probability node vv “fires” (equals 1) student ii : P(vi=1)=logit−1(av⏟slope⋅ψv(parents v)⏟condensation rule−bv⏟intercept) P(v_i = 1) = \text{logit}^{-1}\!\Big(\underbrace{a_v}_{\text{slope}} \cdot \underbrace{\psi_v(\text{parents } v)}_{\text{condensation rule}} - \underbrace{b_v}_{\text{intercept}}\Big) Let us unpack piece: logistic function logit−1(x)=11+e−x\text{logit}^{-1}(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}} transforms real number probability 0 1. standard “link” function logistic regression: input large positive, probability approaches 1. large negative, probability approaches 0. x=0x = 0, probability exactly 0.500.50. slope (also called discrimination parameter) controls sharply probability transitions low high condensed input changes. Think “sensitivity” node: large slope (e.g., av=5a_v = 5) means node good discriminating students prerequisite skills . probability jumps rapidly near 0 near 1. small slope (e.g., av=0.5a_v = 0.5) means node weak discriminator - even students right skills moderately higher probability success. pgdcm code, item slopes stored lambda[j, 1] attribute transition slopes stored theta[k, 1]. constrained positive via truncated normal prior TN(μ,σ,0,∞)\text{TN}(\mu, \sigma, 0, \infty), ensuring skill always means higher probability (never inverted). intercept controls baseline difficulty node - hard “pass” condensation rule (ψv\psi_v) outputs exactly zero. Specifically: ψv=0\psi_v = 0, probability resolves logit−1(−bv)\text{logit}^{-1}(-b_v). large positive bvb_v means node difficult: baseline probability success low (hard guess, hard average student). large negative bvb_v means node easy: baseline probability success high. bv=0b_v = 0, baseline probability exactly 0.50. code, item intercepts lambda[j, 2] attribute intercepts theta[k, 2]. Minus Sign? Notice equation uses av⋅ψv−bva_v \cdot \psi_v - b_v av⋅ψv+bva_v \cdot \psi_v + b_v. minus sign psychometric convention makes bvb_v directly interpretable difficulty: higher bvb_v = harder item. used plus sign, positive bvb_v make item easier, less intuitive. see convention IRT textbooks. node one parent nodes (nodes arrows pointing ). condensation rule ψv\psi_v takes parent values collapses single number logistic regression uses input. condensation rule computed calc_mixed_kernel() function. three options: detail rule next section.

what: 3. The Three Condensation Rules (Pure Discrete Case)
title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: build intuition, let us first consider pure discrete case - parent attributes binary (0 1). extend continuous parents later. Suppose item jj requires skills α1\alpha_1 α2\alpha_2 (indicated 1s corresponding Q-matrix row). DINA rule asks: student possess required skills? ψjDINA=∏k∈parents(j)αikqjk={1if student mastered every required skill0otherwise \psi_j^{\text{DINA}} = \prod_{k \\text{parents}(j)} \alpha_{ik}^{q_{jk}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{student mastered every required skill} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} qjkq_{jk} Q-matrix entry (1 skill kk required item jj, 0 otherwise) αik\alpha_{ik} student ii’s mastery state (0 1) skill kk. Example: Item 5 requires skills AA BB. DINA rule strict: mastering 3 4 required skills treated identically mastering 0 4. partial credit partial mastery. code, computed : full logistic equation, probabilities become: skills present (ψ=1\psi = 1): P=logit−1(⋅1−b)=logit−1(−b)P = \text{logit}^{-1}(\cdot 1 - b) = \text{logit}^{-1}(- b) skill missing (ψ=0\psi = 0): P=logit−1(⋅0−b)=logit−1(−b)P = \text{logit}^{-1}(\cdot 0 - b) = \text{logit}^{-1}(-b) gap two probabilities controlled slope aa. large slope creates wide gap, meaning item can clearly distinguish masters non-masters. DINO rule asks: student possess LEAST ONE required skill? ψjDINO=1−∏k∈parents(j)(1−αik)qjk={1if student mastered least one required skill0if student mastered none required skills \psi_j^{\text{DINO}} = 1 - \prod_{k \\text{parents}(j)} (1 - \alpha_{ik})^{q_{jk}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{student mastered least one required skill} \\ 0 & \text{student mastered none required skills} \end{cases} Example: item requiring skills AA BB: DINO lenient: just one required skills good . models scenarios multiple skills independently provide path correct answer. code: DINM rule asks: fraction required skills student possess? ψjDINM=∑kqjk⋅αik∑kqjk \psi_j^{\text{DINM}} = \frac{\sum_{k} q_{jk} \cdot \alpha_{ik}}{\sum_{k} q_{jk}} produces value 0 1, representing proportion required skills mastered. Example: item requiring skills AA BB: DINM provides partial credit: additional skill smoothly increases probability success. “forgiving” model terms rewarding partial knowledge. code:
code: gate <- (sum_disc == req_disc)   # Are all required discrete skills present? val_dina <- gate                 # 1 if yes, 0 if no dino_gate <- 0.0 if (sum_disc > 0.0) {     dino_gate <- 1.0        # At least one skill is present } val_dino <- dino_gate val_dinm <- sum_total / max(1, sum_input)

what: 6. The Gated Condensation Rule: Handling Mixed Parents
title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: happens node continuous discrete parents? arises naturally Bayesian network continuous root (η\eta) discrete attributes (A1,A2A_1, A_2) point child node. calc_mixed_kernel() function handles gating mechanism: Separate parents continuous parents (indices ≤\leqnrbetaroot) discrete parents (indices >>nrbetaroot). Check gate (specific DINA DINO). gate open, pass continuous signal . gate closed, apply severe penalty (−10-10), drives logistic probability near zero. ψGated-DINA={∑m≤Mrootqjm⋅ηim⏟sum continuous inputsif required discrete skills mastered−10otherwise (gate closed) \psi^{\text{Gated-DINA}} = \begin{cases} \underbrace{\sum_{m \leq M_{\text{root}}} q_{jm} \cdot \eta_{im}}_{\text{sum continuous inputs}} & \text{required discrete skills mastered} \\[8pt] -10 & \text{otherwise (gate closed)} \end{cases} Semantic meaning: student must first pass discrete skills “gatekeeper.” possess every required binary skill continuous ability actually count. Otherwise, amount general ability can help - gate shut. ψGated-DINO={∑m≤Mrootqjm⋅ηimif least one required discrete skill mastered−10otherwise (gate closed) \psi^{\text{Gated-DINO}} = \begin{cases} \sum_{m \leq M_{\text{root}}} q_{jm} \cdot \eta_{im} & \text{least one required discrete skill mastered} \\[8pt] -10 & \text{otherwise (gate closed)} \end{cases} Semantic meaning: student needs one required binary skills unlock gate. skill present, continuous ability flows . what: 1. The Big Picture: Everything is a Bayesian Network
title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: pgdcm model Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) - network nodes connected directional arrows, cycles. two kinds nodes: Attribute nodes (latent/unobserved): represent skills, abilities, competencies directly see. example, “Addition” “Reading Comprehension.” want estimate whether student mastered . Task nodes (observed): represent actual test items (questions). can see whether student answered correctly (1) incorrectly (0). arrows (edges) graph encode dependency: arrow Attribute AA Task TT, means mastery AA influences probability answering TT correctly. arrow Attribute A1A_1 Attribute A2A_2, means A1A_1 prerequisite A2A_2. question every model must answer : Given pattern correct/incorrect responses observe, likely configuration latent skills student? graph acyclic, can always arrange nodes topological order - ordering every parent appears children. pgdcm package enforces automatically via enforce_topo_sort(). ordering critical means can process nodes left right: reach node, parents already defined. nodes ordered : α1,α2,…,αK⏟Attribute nodes,X1,X2,…,XJ⏟Task nodes \underbrace{\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_K}_{\text{Attribute nodes}} \;,\; \underbrace{X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_J}_{\text{Task nodes}} KK number attributes JJ number items.

what: 2. The Universal Equation: Logistic Regression at Every Node
title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: Every node network (whether attribute task) follows fundamental equation. probability node vv “fires” (equals 1) student ii : P(vi=1)=logit−1(av⏟slope⋅ψv(parents v)⏟condensation rule−bv⏟intercept) P(v_i = 1) = \text{logit}^{-1}\!\Big(\underbrace{a_v}_{\text{slope}} \cdot \underbrace{\psi_v(\text{parents } v)}_{\text{condensation rule}} - \underbrace{b_v}_{\text{intercept}}\Big) Let us unpack piece: logistic function logit−1(x)=11+e−x\text{logit}^{-1}(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-x}} transforms real number probability 0 1. standard “link” function logistic regression: input large positive, probability approaches 1. large negative, probability approaches 0. x=0x = 0, probability exactly 0.500.50. slope (also called discrimination parameter) controls sharply probability transitions low high condensed input changes. Think “sensitivity” node: large slope (e.g., av=5a_v = 5) means node good discriminating students prerequisite skills . probability jumps rapidly near 0 near 1. small slope (e.g., av=0.5a_v = 0.5) means node weak discriminator - even students right skills moderately higher probability success. pgdcm code, item slopes stored lambda[j, 1] attribute transition slopes stored theta[k, 1]. constrained positive via truncated normal prior TN(μ,σ,0,∞)\text{TN}(\mu, \sigma, 0, \infty), ensuring skill always means higher probability (never inverted). intercept controls baseline difficulty node - hard “pass” condensation rule (ψv\psi_v) outputs exactly zero. Specifically: ψv=0\psi_v = 0, probability resolves logit−1(−bv)\text{logit}^{-1}(-b_v). large positive bvb_v means node difficult: baseline probability success low (hard guess, hard average student). large negative bvb_v means node easy: baseline probability success high. bv=0b_v = 0, baseline probability exactly 0.50. code, item intercepts lambda[j, 2] attribute intercepts theta[k, 2]. Minus Sign? Notice equation uses av⋅ψv−bva_v \cdot \psi_v - b_v av⋅ψv+bva_v \cdot \psi_v + b_v. minus sign psychometric convention makes bvb_v directly interpretable difficulty: higher bvb_v = harder item. used plus sign, positive bvb_v make item easier, less intuitive. see convention IRT textbooks. node one parent nodes (nodes arrows pointing ). condensation rule ψv\psi_v takes parent values collapses single number logistic regression uses input. condensation rule computed calc_mixed_kernel() function. three options: detail rule next section.

what: 3. The Three Condensation Rules (Pure Discrete Case)
title: Mathematical Foundations of the PGDCM Bayesian Network Model
text: build intuition, let us first consider pure discrete case - parent attributes binary (0 1). extend continuous parents later. Suppose item jj requires skills α1\alpha_1 α2\alpha_2 (indicated 1s corresponding Q-matrix row). DINA rule asks: student possess required skills? ψjDINA=∏k∈parents(j)αikqjk={1if student mastered every required skill0otherwise \psi_j^{\text{DINA}} = \prod_{k \\text{parents}(j)} \alpha_{ik}^{q_{jk}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{student mastered every required skill} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} qjkq_{jk} Q-matrix entry (1 skill kk required item jj, 0 otherwise) αik\alpha_{ik} student ii’s mastery state (0 1) skill kk. Example: Item 5 requires skills AA BB. DINA rule strict: mastering 3 4 required skills treated identically mastering 0 4. partial credit partial mastery. code, computed : full logistic equation, probabilities become: skills present (ψ=1\psi = 1): P=logit−1(⋅1−b)=logit−1(−b)P = \text{logit}^{-1}(\cdot 1 - b) = \text{logit}^{-1}(- b) skill missing (ψ=0\psi = 0): P=logit−1(⋅0−b)=logit−1(−b)P = \text{logit}^{-1}(\cdot 0 - b) = \text{logit}^{-1}(-b) gap two probabilities controlled slope aa. large slope creates wide gap, meaning item can clearly distinguish masters non-masters. DINO rule asks: student possess LEAST ONE required skill? ψjDINO=1−∏k∈parents(j)(1−αik)qjk={1if student mastered least one required skill0if student mastered none required skills \psi_j^{\text{DINO}} = 1 - \prod_{k \\text{parents}(j)} (1 - \alpha_{ik})^{q_{jk}} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{student mastered least one required skill} \\ 0 & \text{student mastered none required skills} \end{cases} Example: item requiring skills AA BB: DINO lenient: just one required skills good . models scenarios multiple skills independently provide path correct answer. code: DINM rule asks: fraction required skills student possess? ψjDINM=∑kqjk⋅αik∑kqjk \psi_j^{\text{DINM}} = \frac{\sum_{k} q_{jk} \cdot \alpha_{ik}}{\sum_{k} q_{jk}} produces value 0 1, representing proportion required skills mastered. Example: item requiring skills AA BB: DINM provides partial credit: additional skill smoothly increases probability success. “forgiving” model terms rewarding partial knowledge. code:
code: gate <- (sum_disc == req_disc)   # Are all required discrete skills present? val_dina <- gate                 # 1 if yes, 0 if no dino_gate <- 0.0 if (sum_disc > 0.0) {     dino_gate <- 1.0        # At least one skill is present } val_dino <- dino_gate val_dinm <- sum_total / max(1, sum_input)

title: Probabilistic Graphical Diagnostic Classification Models
text: PGDCM unified framework building estimating several psychometric models within probabilistic graphical modeling framework (.e., treating psychometric models special cases Bayesian networks, Hidden Markov Models, Partially Observed Markov Decision Processes). Currently supported models include: Diagnostic Classification Models (DCM) dichotomous responses Higher-order Diagnostic Classification Models (HDCM) dichotomous responses Multidimensional Item Response Theory (MIRT) Models dichotomous responses Item Response Theory (IRT) Models dichotomous responses Linear Logistic Structural Equation Models (SEM) continuous responses

title: Probabilistic Graphical Diagnostic Classification Models
text: PGDCM designed provide maximum flexibility three core pillars: Modeling Capabilities, Estimation & Workflow, Architecture & Extensibility.

title: Probabilistic Graphical Diagnostic Classification Models
text: Unified Model Framework - DCM, Higher-Order DCM, IRT, MIRT, SEM treated special cases probabilistic graphical model. graph structure alone determines underlying model - manual model selection flags required. Per-Item Compute Types - Assign different response rules (e.g., DINA, DINO, DINM) individual items within single model. Unlike traditional software forces one global rule, pgdcm lets item carry compensatory, non-compensatory, additive logic. Structural Skill Dependencies - Easily model prerequisite relationships skills (attribute hierarchies) shared latent abilities (higher-order factors). directed graph automatically encodes dependencies estimator. Latent Class Enumeration - Automatically classify examinees 2K2^K possible mastery profiles compute class-level prevalence rates population-level diagnostic reporting.

title: Probabilistic Graphical Diagnostic Classification Models
text: End--End Bayesian Pipeline - single function call orchestrates entire analysis: prior predictive checking, MCMC sampling, convergence diagnostics, posterior predictive checking, result extraction (skill profiles, item parameters, WAIC). Operational Scoring - Lock calibrated item parameters calibration sample score new examinees validated framework. Flexible Prior Specification - Supply custom priors simple (mean, sd) pairs full per-parameter arrays. Omit priors weakly informative defaults, use near-zero variance lock values scoring workflows. Missing Data Handling - Built-Bayesian missing data capabilities. Safely estimate item parameters student profiles even response matrix missing data.

title: Probabilistic Graphical Diagnostic Classification Models
text: Multiple Graph Input Methods - Define models way like: Q-matrix, adjacency matrix, node/edge CSV files, GraphML files, Cytoscape GUI, entirely R code. GUI & Code-Based Graph Building - Build visualize directed graphs interactively using Cytoscape UI, opt completely code-based workflow using provided igraph functions eliminate external dependencies entirely. NIMBLE Extensibility - Built NIMBLE probabilistic programming engine, advanced users can seamlessly swap custom model code, samplers, inference algorithms without leaving package pipeline.

what: Run Integrated Workflow (Prior, MCMC, Posterior) — run_pgdcm_auto
title: Run Integrated Workflow (Prior, MCMC, Posterior) — run_pgdcm_auto
text: Orchestrates complete end--end Bayesian estimation pipeline PGDCM SEM. Computes prior predictive checks, compiles runs Nimble MCMC, assesses convergence, generates posterior predictive checks.

title: Run Integrated Workflow (Prior, MCMC, Posterior) — run_pgdcm_auto
code: run_pgdcm_auto(   config,   estimation_config = list(niter = 1000, nburnin = 100, chains = 2, prior_sims = NULL,     post_sims = NULL),   prefix = NULL,   threshold = 0.5,   return_groups = FALSE,   estimate = TRUE )

title: Run Integrated Workflow (Prior, MCMC, Posterior) — run_pgdcm_auto
text: config Model configuration list build_model_config. estimation_config List parameters controlling MCMC execution (niter, nburnin, chains, prior_sims, post_sims). prior_sims post_sims arguments control number simulated datasets drawn Prior Posterior Predictive Checking respectively. Passing NULL disables checks entirely. Defaults list(niter = 1000, nburnin = 100, chains = 2, prior_sims = NULL, post_sims = NULL). prefix Character. Descriptor prefix used saving generated reports. Defaults timestamped string based model type. threshold Numeric. mastery probability threshold use latent class grouping. Default 0.5. return_groups Logical. TRUE, model groups participants exhaustive latent classes (Caution: Scales exponentially attributes). Default FALSE. estimate Logical. TRUE (default), MCMC estimation performed. FALSE, estimation skipped (e.g., run prior predictive checks).

title: Run Integrated Workflow (Prior, MCMC, Posterior) — run_pgdcm_auto
text: comprehensive list containing: mcmc_out: raw Nimble MCMC output list. samples: cleaned mcmc.list structural NAs removed. mapped_parameters: data.frame summary statistics mapped model parameter names human-readable names. skill_profiles: Generated generate_summary_tables(); x K matrix posterior mean mastery student. item_parameters: Generated generate_summary_tables(); clean table difficulty discrimination parameters per item. group_patterns: Generated generate_summary_tables(); list organizing participants latent classes based 0.5 mastery thresholds. prior_ppc: Results prior predictive check (requested). post_ppc: Results posterior predictive check (requested). WAIC: Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion metric.
